intro.kicker: Topic 22 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: A spring balance measures apparent weight (reaction force), which changes in accelerating frames. A physical balance compares masses by equalising gravitational forces, so it is immune to non-inertial frame effects. NEET targets: (1) spring balance reading in accelerating lifts; (2) ‘false balance’ errors when pan masses are unequal — the true weight is the geometric mean W = √(W₁W₂) (unequal arm length) or arithmetic mean W = (W₁+W₂)/2 (equal arm, unequal pan masses).

expectedQuestions.description: Spring balance in a lift appears roughly every 2–3 years in NEET. Physical balance false-weight calculations appear slightly less frequently but are conceptually tied to ratio problems.

timeRequired.description: Spring balance theory and lift cases — 15 min; physical balance (perfect vs false, two cases) — 15 min; PYQ drills — 15 min.

difficulty.description: Spring balance in non-inertial frames follows directly from the apparent weight table (Topic 13). The false balance derivations require setting up two-equation systems, but the final formulas are compact and memorisable.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics discusses spring scales in lifts. The ‘false balance’ derivation (geometric mean W = √(W₁W₂) for unequal-arm balance) is very specific to the Indian curriculum and is not typically covered in US courses.

bulletNotes: Spring balance reading = R = m(g ± a) depending on elevator direction.
Physical balance perfect: not affected by non-inertial frames — both pans experience same floor acceleration.
False balance case (a): unequal arm lengths L₁ ≠ L₂ with equal pan masses — true weight W = √(W₁W₂).
False balance case (b): equal arm lengths but unequal pan masses m₁ ≠ m₂ — true weight W = (W₁+W₂)/2.

strategySteps.title: Spring Balance in an Elevator
strategySteps.description: The reading R = m(g+a) when going up (or decelerating downward). R = m(g−a) when going down (or decelerating upward). R = 0 in free fall. These are identical to the apparent weight results from Topic 13 (Apparent Weight in Lift).

strategySteps.title: Perfect Physical Balance
strategySteps.description: When a physical balance is perfect (equal arm lengths, equal pan masses), it compares gravitational weight directly. Being in a non-inertial frame does NOT affect the reading: both pans experience the same pseudo-force, so they cancel.

strategySteps.title: False Balance — Two Cases
strategySteps.description: Case (a): Unequal arm lengths. Body weighed twice: W₁ and W₂. True weight W = √(W₁W₂) (geometric mean). Case (b): Equal arm lengths, unequal pan masses. Body weighed twice: W₁ and W₂. True weight W = (W₁+W₂)/2 (arithmetic mean).

cards.title: Spring & Physical Balance — Complete Notes
cards.description: Spring balance lift cases (all 4), physical balance perfect vs false balance two cases, formula derivations, and NEET solved examples.

subtopics.name: Spring Balance in Inertial and Non-Inertial Frames
False Balance Case (a): Unequal Arm Lengths
False Balance Case (b): Unequal Pan Masses

cards.title: Spring Balance — All Four Cases
cards.coreRevision: From OCR page 203: 'Spring balance: When its upper end is fixed with rigid support and body of mass m hung from its lower end.' The reading equals the reaction force R, not the true weight.
points: Lift at rest or uniform velocity: R = mg
cards.example: Body mass 10 kg, lift accelerating up at 2 m/s²: Spring balance reads R = 10×(10+2) = 120 N.

cards.title: Physical Balance — Perfect vs False
cards.coreRevision: From OCR page 203: 'In physical balance actually we compare the mass of body in both the pans. Here we do not calculate the absolute weight of the body.' A perfect balance is unaffected by non-inertial frames: the pseudo-force on both sides is equal and cancels.
points: Perfect physical balance: frame-independent — reads correctly even in an accelerating lift
False balance: unequal arm lengths or unequal pan masses introduce reading error
Distinguishing feature: spring balance IS frame-dependent; physical balance is NOT (when perfect)
cards.example: Weighing on Earth vs Moon with a physical balance gives the same mass reading. With a spring balance, different readings (because g differs).

cards.title: False Balance — True Weight Formulas
cards.coreRevision: From OCR page 203: 'When the masses of the pan are not equal then balance shows the error in measurement.' Two derivation cases give two different true-weight formulas.
points: Two measurements: W₁ (body in left pan), W₂ (body in right pan)
Case (b): Equal arm lengths, unequal pan masses m₁ ≠ m₂
cards.example: Unequal arm: W₁ = 9 N, W₂ = 4 N. True weight = √(9×4) = √36 = 6 N.

curriculum.hintText: Spring balance (scale) in an elevator is standard US AP Physics. Physical balance and false weight derivations are unique to the Indian competitive exam curriculum.

gaps.title: False Balance — Geometric vs Arithmetic Mean
gaps.description: The false balance problem asks: if a balance has unequal arm lengths, what is the true mass? This requires deriving torque equilibrium equations for two configurations. US students will find the formula W = √(W₁W₂) completely new.
bullets: Unequal arm: L₁W = L₂W₁ and L₂W = L₁W₂. Multiply: W² = W₁W₂, so W = √(W₁W₂).
Unequal pan: (W+m₁g)L = m₂gL gives W₁; swapped gives W₂. Average: W = (W₁+W₂)/2.

gaps.title: Physical Balance Frame Independence
gaps.description: US students may not know that a physical balance (two-arm scale) is unaffected by the acceleration of the frame. This is because pseudo-force acts equally on both sides, leaving the torque balance unchanged.
bullets: In a lift accelerating at a, both pans experience pseudo-force m̀a downward. Balance condition: m₁L₁ = m₂L₂ is preserved. Reading unchanged.
This is why a physical balance measures mass (frame-independent), not weight (frame-dependent).

questions.question: A boy of mass 50 kg stands on a spring balance in a lift. If the lift is accelerating downward at 3 m/s², what does the spring balance read? (g = 10 m/s²)
A false balance with unequal arm lengths weighs an object as 9 N on the left pan and 4 N on the right pan. The true weight is:
A physical balance is used to weigh an object in a lift accelerating upward at 5 m/s². The balance reads 2 kg. What is the actual mass of the object?
A false balance with equal arm lengths but unequal pan masses shows W₁ = 5 N and W₂ = 3 N. What is the true weight?

items.title: Recall all four spring balance readings in a lift
items.description: Rest/uniform: mg. Accelerating up: m(g+a). Accelerating down: m(g−a). Free fall: 0. These are direct apparent weight results.

items.title: State why a perfect physical balance is frame-independent
items.description: Pseudo-force acts on both pans equally, so torque balance is unchanged. The balance reads the correct mass regardless of elevator acceleration.

items.title: Memorise both false balance formulas
items.description: Unequal arm lengths: W = √(W₁W₂). Equal arm, unequal pans: W = (W₁+W₂)/2. Know which formula to use in each case.

items.title: Derive the false balance geometric mean from torque equations
items.description: For NEET 2-mark conceptual questions, being able to quote the derivation steps (L₁W₂ = L₂W and L₂W₁ = L₁W → multiply → W² = W₁W₂) is a bonus mark-saver.

items.question: Why does a physical balance still work correctly in an accelerating lift?
items.answer: A physical balance in equilibrium satisfies torque balance: m₁L = m₂L (equal arms). If the lift accelerates at a, the effective gravity becomes (g+a) or (g−a), but this factor multiplies both sides equally. The balance condition m₁ = m₂ is unchanged. So the balance measures the ratio of masses, not the local g.

items.question: Would a spring balance and physical balance give different readings on the Moon?
items.answer: Yes. A spring balance reads weight (mg), which is lower on the Moon (g_Moon ≈ 1.67 m/s²). A physical balance compares masses, so it reads the same mass on the Moon as on Earth. This is the key conceptual difference for NEET.

items.question: In the false balance case (a), why is the result the geometric mean rather than the arithmetic mean?
items.answer: Setting up torque equilibrium twice: W×L₁ = W₁×L₂ and W×L₂ = W₂×L₁. Multiply both equations: W²×L₁L₂ = W₁W₂×L₁L₂. Cancel L₁L₂: W² = W₁W₂, giving W = √(W₁W₂). This is the geometric mean by construction.

grounding_summary: Page 203 contains the complete Spring Balance section and the Physical Balance section including both false-balance cases with their derivations.
OCR confirms the spring balance formula: when the lift accelerates, the reading changes by ±ma.
OCR confirms the physical balance section: true weight = √(W1W2) for unequal arm case and (W1+W2)/2 for equal arm, unequal pan mass case.
grounding_excerpt: Spring balance: When its upper end is fixed with rigid support and body of mass
In physical balance actually we compare the mass of body in both the pans.
When the masses of the pan are not equal then balance shows the error in measurement.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: NCERT discusses apparent weight in lifts and the principle of physical balance as distinct instruments measuring apparent and true weight respectively.

sources.title: NEET UG 2026 Physics Strategy — Weighing Instruments (Edufever)
sources.why_used: Identifies balance and spring scale problems as recurring NEET topics in Newton’s Laws unit, recommending formula memorisation for false balance cases.

review.notes: Content grounded in OCR page 203. Three verbatim excerpts from the spring balance and physical balance sections. Both false-balance cases and their formulas (geometric mean and arithmetic mean) derived and verified from OCR text.
